abstracts.title: Insurance Requirements
abstracts.desc: Master policy and unit owner coverage obligations under the Declaration and Bylaws.
abstracts.subtitle: The insurance provisions from the Declaration, Bylaws, and Articles of Incorporation outline coverage requirements for both the Association and individual unit owners.

blocks.heading: Master Insurance Policy
blocks.text: The Lakewest Condominiums Association is responsible for maintaining a master insurance policy covering the building and shared elements. This policy protects the structure itself, but it does not cover what’s inside your unit.

cards.label: Property Insurance
cards.text: Coverage for common elements and limited common elements.

cards.label: General Liability
cards.text: Protection against lawsuits related to property damage or personal injury.

cards.label: D&O Insurance
cards.text: Legal defense and liability coverage for the Board of Directors.

cards.label: Fidelity Bond
cards.text: Coverage for financial losses due to fraud, embezzlement, or theft involving Association funds.

blocks.text: The Board of Directors is responsible for reviewing and renewing insurance policies annually to ensure compliance with legal requirements and lender standards.

blocks.heading: Individual Unit Insurance (HO-6 Policy)
blocks.text: Each unit owner is required to maintain an HO-6 homeowner’s insurance policy. This is your personal coverage—it picks up where the Association’s master policy stops. Owners must provide proof of insurance upon request by the Board or Property Manager.

cards.label: Interior & Improvements
cards.text: Interior structures and improvements within the unit.

cards.label: Personal Belongings
cards.text: Personal belongings and personal liability coverage.

cards.label: Loss Assessment
cards.text: Coverage for the owner’s share of Association claims or damage.

cards.label: Deductible
cards.text: Responsibility for deductibles on claims originating within the unit.

action.label: If you don’t have an HO-6 policy yet
action.title: Get Your HO-6 Coverage Set Up
steps: Contact your insurance provider and request an HO-6 condominium policy.
Ensure it covers interior improvements, personal belongings, personal liability, and loss assessments.
Ask about deductible coverage—if a claim originates in your unit, you’re responsible for the Association’s master policy deductible.
Provide proof of insurance to the Property Manager when requested.

blocks.heading: Damage Responsibility & Deductibles
blocks.text: Understanding who pays when something goes wrong is one of the most important parts of living in a condominium. The general rule: if the damage starts in your unit, you’re on the hook.
items: If damage originates within a unit (e.g., water leaks, electrical issues), the unit owner is responsible for the deductible and repair costs.
If damage affects common areas or multiple units, the Association’s policy applies, but owners may be assessed a portion of the costs.
The Association may special assess owners for any insurance shortfalls or uncovered claims.

blocks.heading: Modification Impact on Insurance
blocks.text: Planning a renovation? Your project may affect the Association’s insurance coverage. Unauthorized work that creates safety risks can result in fines—and if something goes wrong, you may not be covered.
items: Any structural modification to a unit requires Board approval and may impact insurance coverage.
Owners must ensure their contractors carry proper liability insurance for renovations.
Unauthorized modifications that cause damage or safety risks may result in fines or mandated repairs.

crossLinks.label: How to submit an ACC request

takeaways: The Association’s master policy covers the building and common areas. It does not cover anything inside your unit.
You need your own HO-6 policy—it covers your interior, your belongings, your liability, and deductibles the Association may pass through to you.
If damage starts in your unit and affects others, you pay the deductible and repair costs. Your HO-6 policy is what protects you here.
Planning renovations? Get Board approval first. Unauthorized work can void coverage and trigger fines.
Keep your policy current and provide proof to the Property Manager when asked.

abstracts.title: Maintenance Responsibilities
abstracts.desc: Owner and Association upkeep obligations, emergency procedures, and funding.
abstracts.subtitle: The Lakewest Condominiums Association enforces maintenance responsibilities to preserve property value, ensure safe living conditions, and clearly define owner vs. Association responsibilities.

blocks.heading: Unit Owner Responsibilities
blocks.text: You maintain everything inside your walls. The Association handles everything outside them. Here’s what falls on you:
items: Interior components such as walls, floors, ceilings, and fixtures.
Appliances, heating, and cooling systems within their unit.
Windows, doors, and balconies, except when damage results from an Association-covered event.
Plumbing and electrical components servicing only their unit.
Pest control within their unit.

crossLinks.label: Make sure your HO-6 covers this

blocks.heading: Association Responsibilities
blocks.text: The Association is responsible for maintaining all shared and building-wide elements—the parts of the property that serve everyone.

cards.label: Common Areas
cards.text: Walkways, driveways, landscaped spaces, and retaining walls.

cards.label: Limited Common Areas
cards.text: Shared spaces such as hallways and stairwells.

cards.label: Building & Roofs
cards.text: Building structures and roofs to ensure weather protection.

cards.label: Utility Systems
cards.text: Plumbing, electrical conduits, and systems servicing multiple units.

cards.label: Safety Systems
cards.text: Fire safety equipment and security systems.

blocks.heading: Emergency Repairs
blocks.text: Security and fire safety take precedence, and the Association may take immediate action in case of hazards.

pullQuote.text: The Association has the right to enter a unit to perform emergency repairs affecting common elements or neighboring units. Owners will be billed if the issue originated from their negligence.
pullQuote.caption: Emergency access provision

pullQuote.text: Owners must report maintenance issues to the Property Manager within 48 hours of noticing a problem. Failure to report damages affecting other units may result in financial liability.
pullQuote.caption: Reporting requirement

action.label: If you discover a maintenance emergency
action.title: What to Do When Something Goes Wrong
steps: Contact the Property Manager immediately—you have 48 hours to report, but sooner is better.
If there’s an active water leak, shut off the supply valve in your unit if you can safely do so.
Document the damage with photos before any cleanup.
File a claim with your HO-6 insurance provider if the issue originated in your unit.
Cooperate with Association access—they may need to enter your unit to stop damage from spreading.

blocks.heading: Monthly Assessments & Reserve Funds
blocks.text: Maintenance costs are covered through monthly HOA fees. These fund day-to-day upkeep and build reserves for larger projects down the road.
items: Routine upkeep of shared spaces and infrastructure.
Long-term reserves for major repairs like roof replacement or resurfacing of parking areas.
Special assessments may be imposed for unexpected major expenses.

blocks.heading: Individual Repair Costs
blocks.text: If a maintenance issue is traced back to an owner’s negligence or misuse, they must cover repair costs. Owners may be required to carry HO-6 insurance to cover damages originating from their unit.

blocks.heading: Failure to Maintain Units
items: Owners who neglect their units (e.g., failing to address water leaks) may be fined or assessed for damages.
The Association may conduct repairs and bill the owner if the problem affects other units or common areas.

blocks.heading: Common Area Violations
items: Unauthorized modifications to limited common areas (e.g., adding structures, altering landscaping) are subject to fines.
Non-compliant structures may require removal at the owner’s expense.

crossLinks.label: Check if your change needs ACC approval

blocks.text: If an owner repeatedly fails to comply with maintenance responsibilities, the Association may take legal action or place a lien on the property for unpaid repair costs.

takeaways: You maintain everything inside your walls. The HOA handles the building envelope, common areas, and shared systems.
Report problems to the Property Manager within 48 hours. Waiting longer can make you financially liable for spreading damage.
The Association can enter your unit in an emergency without notice. Cooperate—it protects everyone.
Your monthly HOA fees fund routine upkeep and reserves. Special assessments cover the big stuff.
Neglecting maintenance can lead to fines, forced repairs billed to you, or a lien on your property.

crossLinks.label: Get your HO-6 policy set up

abstracts.title: ACC Requests
abstracts.desc: Architectural Control Committee approval process for modifications to units and common areas.
abstracts.subtitle: The Architectural Control Committee ensures that exterior modifications, improvements, or structural changes comply with the Association’s Declaration, Bylaws, and Rules & Regulations.

blocks.heading: Changes Requiring ACC Approval
blocks.text: If you’re planning any work that changes the look or structure of your unit—even something as simple as replacing a window—you likely need approval first. ACC approval is required for any modification that affects the external appearance or structure of a unit, including but not limited to:
items: All alterations or modifications to a Unit—including changes to flooring, plumbing, electrical systems, exterior elements, or any modifications impacting Common or Limited Common Elements.
Structural modifications such as room additions and deck extensions.
Exterior painting or material changes.
Landscaping alterations beyond standard maintenance.
Installation of solar panels, satellite dishes, or security systems.
Window or door replacements that differ from the original design.
Fencing, enclosures, or privacy screens.
HVAC system modifications such as exterior air conditioning units.

cards.label: 01 — Submit Request
cards.text: Submit a written ACC request form with detailed plans, material samples, and estimated timelines at least 30 days before the intended start date.

cards.label: 02 — ACC Review
cards.text: The ACC reviews the request for compliance with Lakewest governing documents. The Board of Directors has the final decision on disputed applications.

cards.label: 03 — Decision
cards.text: Owners receive a written decision within 30 days of submission. Approved requests must be completed within six months unless an extension is granted.

pullQuote.text: Requests must be submitted at least 30 days before the intended start date. Approved modifications must be completed within 6 months.
pullQuote.caption: ACC timeline requirements

action.label: Before you start any project
action.title: Your Pre-Project Checklist
steps: Check the requirements list above—does your project need ACC approval? When in doubt, assume yes.
Prepare your submission: detailed plans, material samples, color swatches, contractor info, and your timeline.
Submit the ACC request form at least 30 days before you want to start work.
Wait for written approval before hiring contractors or purchasing materials.
Once approved, complete the work within 6 months. Need more time? Request an extension.
Make sure your contractor carries proper liability insurance—this affects your coverage too.

blocks.heading: Conformity with Community Aesthetic
items: Modifications must align with existing architectural styles and colors.
Prohibited modifications include bright, non-standard exterior colors and materials that significantly alter the building’s original design.

blocks.heading: Structural and Safety Compliance
items: All modifications must comply with City of Seattle building codes and require necessary permits.
Work must be completed by licensed contractors when applicable.

blocks.heading: Maintenance Responsibility
blocks.text: Owners remain responsible for maintaining approved modifications. If modifications lead to damage or deterioration, the Association may require repairs at the owner’s expense.

crossLinks.label: Full list of owner maintenance duties

blocks.heading: Fines & Reversal Orders
items: Unauthorized modifications may result in fines ranging from $250–$1,000.
Owners may be required to remove or reverse unapproved modifications at their own cost.

pullQuote.text: Unauthorized modifications may result in fines of $250–$1,000. Continued non-compliance may lead to legal action or a lien placed against the property.
pullQuote.caption: ACC violation penalties

takeaways: Get approval before you start—not after. Skipping this step can cost you $250–$1,000 in fines plus the cost of undoing the work.
Submit your request at least 30 days early. Include plans, materials, and a timeline.
Finish approved work within 6 months or request an extension.
Your contractor needs liability insurance. If they don’t have it, it can affect your coverage and the Association’s.
You’re responsible for maintaining any modifications you make—indefinitely.

crossLinks.label: What happens if you don’t maintain your unit

abstracts.title: Board of Directors
abstracts.desc: Governance structure, elections, authority, and current board composition.
abstracts.subtitle: The Board of Directors serves as the principal governing body for the Lakewest Condominium Association, responsible for administration, maintenance, financial oversight, and rule enforcement.

cards.label: Board Size
cards.text: Five (5) directors elected from among unit owners.

cards.label: Townhouse Rep
cards.text: At least one director must be a Townhouse owner if eligible and willing.

cards.label: Entity Owners
cards.text: Directors must be natural persons but may act through designated representatives if the unit is owned by an entity.

blocks.heading: Election & Terms
blocks.text: Elections are held annually at the members’ meeting or by mail ballot. Members may nominate additional candidates beyond those proposed by the Board.
items: Directors serve staggered two-year terms—three seats expire in odd years, two in even years.
Elections are held annually at the members’ meeting or by mail ballot.

pullQuote.text: A director may be removed with or without cause by a majority vote of apartment owners at a duly called meeting.
pullQuote.caption: Director removal threshold

blocks.text: During the Developer’s control period, they may appoint and remove directors at will. Directors with three unexcused consecutive absences may be removed by Board action.

action.label: Want to get involved?
action.title: How to Run for the Board
steps: You must be a unit owner (or designated representative of an entity owner).
Nominations can be made at the annual meeting or submitted in advance to the Board.
Elections are held by vote at the annual meeting or by mail ballot.
Directors serve two-year terms. Seats are staggered so the full Board doesn’t turn over at once.

blocks.heading: Meetings & Decision-Making
items: Regular meetings require no notice; special meetings require five days’ notice.
A majority of directors constitutes a quorum.
Actions may be taken without a meeting via unanimous written consent or remote participation.

blocks.heading: Officers of the Board
blocks.text: Officers are elected annually by the Board and serve one-year terms. The Board may appoint assistants and delegate duties as needed.

cards.label: President
cards.text: Leads meetings and executes official business.

cards.label: Vice President
cards.text: Acts in the President’s absence.

cards.label: Secretary
cards.text: Keeps records and sends notices.

cards.label: Treasurer
cards.text: Oversees financials and accounts.

blocks.heading: Compensation & Liability
blocks.text: Directors serve without compensation. Indemnification is provided for actions taken in good faith within the scope of their authority. Liability insurance may be maintained for directors and officers.

blocks.heading: Roles & Responsibilities
blocks.text: The Board’s authority touches nearly every aspect of life at Lakewest. They set the rules, manage the money, and make decisions on behalf of all owners.
items: Enforcing rules and regulations and hearing violations through a formal process.
Preparing and adopting budgets and levying assessments.
Approving expenditures and managing reserve accounts.
Representing the Association in legal and administrative matters.
Authorizing repairs, improvements, and common area maintenance.

crossLinks.label: How the Board reviews ACC requests

pullQuote.text: Amendments to Bylaws or Declaration require 67% owner approval, and in some cases, 51% mortgagee consent.
pullQuote.caption: Amendment threshold

blocks.text: The Board can adopt and amend rules, impose fines, and initiate legal action for violations. A due-process hearing procedure is in place for alleged rule violations. The Board cannot override rules adopted by a majority of owners.

takeaways: Five elected directors govern Lakewest with staggered two-year terms. You can run for a seat at any annual meeting.
Any director can be removed by a majority vote of owners—no specific cause is required.
The Board manages finances, enforces rules, approves modifications, and represents the Association legally.
Meetings are open. Regular meetings don’t require advance notice—you can attend.
Changing the governing documents requires 67% of owners to agree. Some changes also need mortgagee consent.
Directors serve without pay and are protected by indemnification when acting in good faith.

abstracts.title: Resale Certificate
abstracts.desc: Legally mandated disclosures required for the sale or transfer of any unit.
abstracts.subtitle: A Resale Certificate is required for the sale or transfer of any unit within Lakewest Condominiums. It provides critical financial and governance disclosures to prospective buyers.

blocks.heading: Purpose & Legal Requirement
blocks.text: The Resale Certificate ensures that buyers receive complete, accurate, and current information regarding the unit and Association operations before closing. It is required by Washington state law (RCW 64.34.425) and supported by the Lakewest governing documents.

blocks.heading: Who Prepares the Certificate?
blocks.text: The Board of Directors or its authorized agent (e.g., a management company) prepares the Resale Certificate upon request.

action.label: If you’re selling your unit
action.title: How to Request a Resale Certificate
steps: Contact the Property Manager or Board to request a Resale Certificate.
Allow time for preparation—request well before your closing date.
The Association may charge a reasonable fee for preparation.
Review the certificate for accuracy before it goes to the buyer.
The buyer receives the certificate before closing and has the right to review it.

blocks.heading: Governance & Membership
items: Confirmation of the unit owner’s membership in the Association.
Voting rights and designation of voting representative.

blocks.heading: Financial Information
items: Monthly assessments and outstanding balances.
Recent and upcoming special assessments.
Approved annual budget and reserves.

blocks.heading: Legal & Enforcement Disclosures
items: Any pending litigation involving the Association.
Any known violations of governing documents by the unit.
Liens due to unpaid assessments.

blocks.heading: Additional Disclosures
items: Summary of Association’s insurance policies (casualty, liability, etc.).
Summary or copy of current rules, including leasing policies and use restrictions.
Description of common and limited common areas associated with the unit.
Summary of how governing documents may be amended.

blocks.heading: Importance of Compliance
blocks.text: The Resale Certificate protects both the buyer and the Association by ensuring full disclosure of important information. It must be prepared and delivered prior to the closing of any unit sale.

takeaways: Selling your unit? You need a Resale Certificate before closing. Request it from the Property Manager or Board early.
The certificate gives buyers a complete picture—finances, insurance, rules, legal issues, and governance.
The Association can charge a fee for preparing it. Budget for this in your selling costs.
An incomplete or missing certificate can delay or cancel your sale. Don’t skip it.

crossLinks.label: Who to contact on the Board
Assessment info included in the certificate

abstracts.title: Quick Reference
abstracts.desc: Key numbers, deadlines, and thresholds from all governing documents in one place.
abstracts.subtitle: Every important number, deadline, and threshold from across all five governing document abstracts — consolidated into a single reference page.

cards.label: ACC Submission
cards.text: Submit requests at least 30 days before your planned start date.

cards.label: ACC Completion
cards.text: Approved modifications must be completed within 6 months.

cards.label: Maintenance Reporting
cards.text: Report issues to the Property Manager within 48 hours of discovery.

cards.label: Special Meetings
cards.text: Five days’ notice required for special Board meetings.

cards.label: ACC Violations
cards.text: $250–$1,000 for unauthorized modifications.

cards.label: Reversal Costs
cards.text: Owners may be required to remove unapproved work at their own expense.

cards.label: Maintenance Neglect
cards.text: Association may conduct repairs and bill the owner directly.

cards.label: Legal Escalation
cards.text: Continued non-compliance may result in liens or legal action.

cards.label: Remove a Director
cards.text: Majority vote of apartment owners at a duly called meeting.

cards.label: Amend Bylaws
cards.text: 67% owner approval required.

cards.label: Amend Declaration
cards.text: 67% owner approval, plus 51% mortgagee consent in some cases.

cards.label: Board Quorum
cards.text: Majority of directors constitutes a quorum.

cards.label: Directors
cards.text: 5 elected directors from among unit owners.

cards.label: Terms
cards.text: Staggered 2-year terms — 3 seats in odd years, 2 in even years.

cards.label: Officers
cards.text: President, Vice President, Secretary, Treasurer — elected annually.

cards.label: Compensation
cards.text: Directors and Officers serve without pay.

cards.label: Association Policy
cards.text: Master policy covers building structure, common elements, liability, D&O, and fidelity bond.

cards.label: Owner Policy (HO-6)
cards.text: Required for interior, belongings, liability, loss assessment, and deductibles.

cards.label: Damage Source Rule
cards.text: If damage originates in your unit, you pay the deductible and repair costs.

cards.label: Contractor Insurance
cards.text: Your contractors must carry liability insurance for any renovation work.

takeaways: Submit ACC requests 30 days early. Complete approved work within 6 months.
Report maintenance issues within 48 hours — especially if they affect other units.
Unauthorized modifications can cost you $250–$1,000 in fines, plus removal costs.
Amending governing documents requires 67% owner approval.
You must carry an HO-6 policy. If damage starts in your unit, you pay.
Directors serve 2-year terms and can be removed by majority vote.
